verses.book: Sermons on Selected Lessons of the New Testament.
verses.text: But inasmuch as the grandfathers, and all the other progenitors which they enumerate, are different, and in the very number of the generations, the one has more, and the other fewer, Joseph is plainly shown hereby to have had two fathers.

verses.book: Sermons on Selected Lessons of the New Testament.
verses.text: Now having disposed of the cavil of this question, forasmuch as clear reason has shown that it may happen that he who has begotten a child may be one father, and he who has adopted him another: supposing two fathers, it is nothing strange if the grandfathers and the great grandfathers, and the rest in the line upwards which are enumerated, should be different as coming from different fathers.

verses.book: Sermons on Selected Lessons of the New Testament.
verses.text: And let not the law of adoption seem to you to be foreign to our Scriptures, and that, as if it were recognised only in the practice of human laws, it cannot fall in with the authority of the divine books.

verses.book: Sermons on Selected Lessons of the New Testament.
verses.text: For it is a thing established of old time, and frequently heard of in the Ecclesiastical books—that not only the natural way of birth, but the free choice of the will also, should give birth to a child.

verses.book: Sermons on Selected Lessons of the New Testament.
verses.text: For women, if they had no children of their own, used to adopt children born of their husbands by their hand-maids, and even oblige their husbands to give them children in this way; as Sarah, Rachel, and Leah.

verses.book: Sermons on Selected Lessons of the New Testament.
verses.text: And in doing this the husbands did not commit adultery, in that they obeyed their wives in that matter which had regard to conjugal duty, according to what the Apostle saith: “The wife hath not power of her own body, but the husband; and likewise also the husband hath not power of his own body, but the wife.”

verses.book: Sermons on Selected Lessons of the New Testament.
verses.text: Moses too, who was born of a Hebrew mother and was exposed, was adopted by Pharaoh’s daughter.

verses.book: Sermons on Selected Lessons of the New Testament.
verses.text: There were not then indeed the same forms of law as now, but the choice of the will was taken for the rule of law, as the Apostle saith also in another place, “The Gentiles which have not the law, do by nature the things contained in the law.”

verses.book: Sermons on Selected Lessons of the New Testament.
verses.text: But if it is permitted to women to make those their children to whom they have not given birth, why should it not be allowed men to do so too with those whom they have not begotten of their body, but of the love of adoption.

verses.book: Sermons on Selected Lessons of the New Testament.
verses.text: For we read that the patriarch Jacob even, the father of so many children, made his grandchildren, the sons of Joseph, his own children, in these words: “These too shall be mine, and they shall receive the land with their brethren, and those which thou begettest after them shall be thine.”

verses.book: Sermons on Selected Lessons of the New Testament.
verses.text: But it will be said, perhaps, that this word “adoption” is not found in the Holy Scriptures.

verses.book: Sermons on Selected Lessons of the New Testament.
verses.text: As though it were of any importance by what name it is called, when the thing itself is there—for a woman to have a child to whom she has not given birth, or a man a child whom he has not begotten.

verses.book: Sermons on Selected Lessons of the New Testament.
verses.text: And he may, without any opposition from me, refuse to call Joseph adopted, provided he grant that he may have been the son of a man of whose body he was not born.

verses.book: Sermons on Selected Lessons of the New Testament.
verses.text: Yet the Apostle Paul does continually use this very word “adoption,” and that to express a great mystery.

verses.book: Sermons on Selected Lessons of the New Testament.
verses.text: For though Scripture testifies that our Lord Jesus Christ is the only Son of God, it says, that the brethren and coheirs whom He hath vouchsafed to have, are made so by a kind of adoption through Divine grace.

verses.book: Sermons on Selected Lessons of the New Testament.
verses.text: “When,” saith he, “the fulness of time was come, God sent forth His Son, made of a woman, made under the law, to redeem them that were under the law, that we might receive the adoption of sons.”

verses.book: Sermons on Selected Lessons of the New Testament.
verses.text: And in another place: “We groan within ourselves, waiting for the adoption, to wit, the redemption of our body.”

verses.book: Sermons on Selected Lessons of the New Testament.
verses.text: And again, when he was speaking of the Jews, “I could wish that myself were accursed from Christ for my brethren, my kinsmen according to the flesh; who are Israelites, to whom pertaineth the adoption, and the glory, and the testaments, and the giving of the law; whose are the fathers, and of whom as concerning the flesh Christ came, Who is over all, God blessed for ever.”

verses.book: Sermons on Selected Lessons of the New Testament.
verses.text: Where he shows, that the word “adoption,” or at least the thing which it signifies, was of ancient use among the Jews, just as was the Testament and the giving of the Law, which he mentions together with it.

verses.book: Sermons on Selected Lessons of the New Testament.
verses.text: Added to this; there is another way peculiar to the Jews, in which a man might be the son of another of whom he was not born according to the flesh.

verses.book: Sermons on Selected Lessons of the New Testament.
verses.text: For kinsmen used to marry the wives of their next of kin, who died without children, to raise up seed to him that was deceased.

verses.book: Sermons on Selected Lessons of the New Testament.
verses.text: So then he who was thus born was both his son of whom he was born, and his in whose line of succession he was born.

verses.book: Sermons on Selected Lessons of the New Testament.
verses.text: All this has been said, lest any one, thinking it impossible for two fathers to be mentioned properly for one man, should imagine that either of the Evangelists who have narrated the generations of the Lord are to be, by an impious calumny, charged so to say with a lie; especially when we may see that we are warned against this by their very words.

verses.book: Sermons on Selected Lessons of the New Testament.
verses.text: For Matthew, who is understood to make mention of that father of whom Joseph was born, enumerates the generations thus: “This one begat the other,” so as to come to what he says at the end, “Jacob begat Joseph.”

verses.book: Sermons on Selected Lessons of the New Testament.
verses.text: But Luke—because he cannot properly be said to be begotten who is made a child either by adoption, or who is born in the succession of the deceased, of her who was his wife—did not say, “Heli begat Joseph,” or “Joseph whom Heli begat,” but “Who was the son of Heli,” whether by adoption, or as being born of the next of kin in the succession of one deceased.

verses.book: Sermons on Selected Lessons of the New Testament.
verses.text: Enough has now been said to show that the question, why the generations are reckoned through Joseph and not through Mary, ought not to perplex us; for as she was a mother without carnal desire, so was he a father without any carnal intercourse.

verses.book: Sermons on Selected Lessons of the New Testament.
verses.text: Let then the generations ascend and descend through him.

verses.book: Sermons on Selected Lessons of the New Testament.
verses.text: And let us not exclude him from being a father, because he had none of this carnal desire.

verses.book: Sermons on Selected Lessons of the New Testament.
verses.text: Let his greater purity only confirm rather his relationship of father, lest the holy Mary herself reproach us.

verses.book: Sermons on Selected Lessons of the New Testament.
verses.text: For she would not put her own name before her husband; but said, “Thy father and I have sought Thee sorrowing.”

verses.book: Sermons on Selected Lessons of the New Testament.
verses.text: Let not then these perverse murmurers do that which the chaste spouse of Joseph did not.

verses.book: Sermons on Selected Lessons of the New Testament.
verses.text: Let us reckon then through Joseph, because as he is in chastity a husband, so is he in chastity a father.

verses.book: Sermons on Selected Lessons of the New Testament.
verses.text: And let us put the man before the woman, according to the order of nature and the law of God.

verses.book: Sermons on Selected Lessons of the New Testament.
verses.text: For if we should cast him aside and leave her, he would say, and say with reason, “Why have you excluded me?

verses.book: Sermons on Selected Lessons of the New Testament.
verses.text: Why do not the generations ascend and descend through me?”

verses.book: Sermons on Selected Lessons of the New Testament.
verses.text: Shall we say to him, “Because thou didst not beget Him by the operation of thy flesh?”

verses.book: Sermons on Selected Lessons of the New Testament.
verses.text: Surely he will answer, “And is it by the operation of the flesh that the Virgin bare Him?

verses.book: Sermons on Selected Lessons of the New Testament.
verses.text: What the Holy Spirit wrought, He wrought for both.”

verses.book: Sermons on Selected Lessons of the New Testament.
verses.text: “Being a just man,” saith the Gospel.

verses.book: Sermons on Selected Lessons of the New Testament.
verses.text: The husband then was just and the woman just.

verses.book: Sermons on Selected Lessons of the New Testament.
verses.text: The Holy Spirit reposing in the justice of them both, gave to both a Son.

verses.book: Sermons on Selected Lessons of the New Testament.
verses.text: In that sex which is by nature fitted to give birth, He wrought that birth which was for the husband also.

verses.book: Sermons on Selected Lessons of the New Testament.
verses.text: And therefore doth the Angel bid them both give the Child a name, and hereby is the authority of both parents established.

verses.book: Sermons on Selected Lessons of the New Testament.
verses.text: For when Zacharias was yet dumb, the mother gave a name to her newborn son.

verses.book: Sermons on Selected Lessons of the New Testament.
verses.text: And when they who were present “made signs to his father what he would have him called, he took a writing-table and wrote” the name which she had already pronounced.

verses.book: Sermons on Selected Lessons of the New Testament.
verses.text: So to Mary too the Angel saith, “Behold, thou shalt conceive a Son, and shalt call His name Jesus.”

verses.book: Sermons on Selected Lessons of the New Testament.
verses.text: And to Joseph also he saith, “Joseph, thou son of David, fear not to take unto thee Mary thy wife; for That which is conceived in her is of the Holy Ghost.

verses.book: Sermons on Selected Lessons of the New Testament.
verses.text: And she shall bring forth a Son, and thou shalt call His name Jesus, for He shall save His people from their sins.”

verses.book: Sermons on Selected Lessons of the New Testament.
verses.text: Again it is said, “And she brought forth a Son to him,” by which he is established to be a father, not in the flesh indeed, but in love.

verses.book: Sermons on Selected Lessons of the New Testament.
verses.text: Let us then acknowledge him to be a father, as in truth he is.

verses.book: Sermons on Selected Lessons of the New Testament.
verses.text: For most advisedly and most wisely do the Evangelists reckon through him, whether Matthew in descending from Abraham down to Christ, or Luke in ascending from Christ through Abraham up to God.

verses.book: Sermons on Selected Lessons of the New Testament.
verses.text: The one reckons in a descending, the other in an ascending order; but both through Joseph.

verses.book: Sermons on Selected Lessons of the New Testament.
verses.text: Because he is the father.

verses.book: Sermons on Selected Lessons of the New Testament.
verses.text: How the father?

verses.book: Sermons on Selected Lessons of the New Testament.
verses.text: Because he is the more undeniably a father in proportion as he is more chastely so.

verses.book: Sermons on Selected Lessons of the New Testament.
verses.text: He was thought, it is true, to be the father of our Lord Jesus Christ in another way: that is, as other parents are according to a fleshly birth, and not through the fruitfulness of a wholly spiritual love.

verses.book: Sermons on Selected Lessons of the New Testament.
verses.text: For Luke said, “Who was supposed to be the father of Jesus.”

verses.book: Sermons on Selected Lessons of the New Testament.
verses.text: Why supposed?

verses.book: Sermons on Selected Lessons of the New Testament.
verses.text: Because men’s thoughts and suppositions were directed to what is usually the case with men.

verses.book: Sermons on Selected Lessons of the New Testament.
verses.text: The Lord then was not of the seed of Joseph, though He was supposed to be; yet nevertheless the Son of the Virgin Mary, who is also the Son of God, was born to Joseph, the fruit of his piety and love.

verses.book: Sermons on Selected Lessons of the New Testament.
verses.text: But why does St Matthew reckon in a descending, and Luke in an ascending order?

verses.book: Sermons on Selected Lessons of the New Testament.
verses.text: I pray you give attentive ear to what the Lord may help me to say on this matter; with your minds now at ease, and disembarrassed from all the perplexity of these cavillings.

verses.book: Sermons on Selected Lessons of the New Testament.
verses.text: Matthew descends through his generations, to signify our Lord Jesus Christ descending to bear our sins, that in the seed of Abraham all nations might be blessed.

verses.book: Sermons on Selected Lessons of the New Testament.
verses.text: Wherefore, he does not begin with Adam, for from him is the whole race of mankind.

verses.book: Sermons on Selected Lessons of the New Testament.
verses.text: Nor with Noe, because from his family again, after the flood, descended the whole human race.

verses.book: Sermons on Selected Lessons of the New Testament.
verses.text: Nor could the man Christ Jesus, as descended from Adam, from whom all men are descended, bear upon the fulfilment of prophecy; nor, again, as descended from Noe, from whom also all men are descended; but only as descended from Abraham, who at that time was chosen, that all nations should be blessed in his seed, when the earth was now full of nations.

verses.book: Sermons on Selected Lessons of the New Testament.
verses.text: But Luke reckons in an ascending order, and does not begin to enumerate the generations from the beginning of the account of our Lord’s birth, but from that place, where he relates His Baptism by John.

verses.book: Sermons on Selected Lessons of the New Testament.
verses.text: Now, as in the incarnation of the Lord, the sins of the human race are taken upon Him to be borne, so in the consecration of His Baptism are they taken on Him to be expiated.

verses.book: Sermons on Selected Lessons of the New Testament.
verses.text: Accordingly, St. Matthew, as representing His descent to bear our sins, enumerates the generations in a descending order; but the other, as representing the expiation of sins, not His own, of course, but our sins, enumerates them in an ascending order.

verses.book: Sermons on Selected Lessons of the New Testament.
verses.text: Again, St. Matthew descends through Solomon, by whose mother David sinned; St. Luke ascends through Nathan another son of the same David, through whom he was purged from his sin.

verses.book: Sermons on Selected Lessons of the New Testament.
verses.text: For we read, that Nathan was sent to him to reprove him, and that he might through repentance be healed.

verses.book: Sermons on Selected Lessons of the New Testament.
verses.text: Both Evangelists meet together in David; the one in descending, the other in ascending; and from David to Abraham, or from Abraham to David, there is no difference in any one generation.

verses.book: Sermons on Selected Lessons of the New Testament.
verses.text: And so Christ, both the Son of David and the Son of Abraham, comes up to God.

verses.book: Sermons on Selected Lessons of the New Testament.
verses.text: For to God must we be brought back, when renewed in Baptism, from the abolition of sins.

verses.book: Sermons on Selected Lessons of the New Testament.
verses.text: Now, in the generations which Matthew enumerates, the predominant number is forty.

verses.book: Sermons on Selected Lessons of the New Testament.
verses.text: For it is a custom of the Holy Scriptures, not to reckon what is over and above certain round numbers.

verses.book: Sermons on Selected Lessons of the New Testament.
verses.text: For thus it is said to be four hundred years, after which the people of Israel went out of Egypt, whereas it is four hundred and thirty.

verses.book: Sermons on Selected Lessons of the New Testament.
verses.text: And so here the one generation, which exceeds the fortieth, does not take away the predominance of that number.

verses.book: Sermons on Selected Lessons of the New Testament.
verses.text: Now this number signifies the life wherein we labour in this world, as long as we are absent from the Lord, during which the temporal dispensation of the preaching of the truth is necessary.

verses.book: Sermons on Selected Lessons of the New Testament.
verses.text: For the number ten, by which the perfection of blessedness is signified, multiplied four times, because of the fourfold divisions of the seasons, and the fourfold divisions of the world, will make the number forty.

verses.book: Sermons on Selected Lessons of the New Testament.
verses.text: Wherefore Moses and Elias, and the Mediator Himself, our Lord Jesus Christ, fasted forty days, because in the time of this life, continence from the enticements of the body is necessary.

verses.book: Sermons on Selected Lessons of the New Testament.
verses.text: Forty years also did the people wander in the wilderness.

verses.book: Sermons on Selected Lessons of the New Testament.
verses.text: Forty days the waters of the flood lasted.

verses.book: Sermons on Selected Lessons of the New Testament.
verses.text: Forty days after His resurrection did the Lord converse with the disciples, persuading them of the reality of His risen body, whereby He showed that in this life, “wherein we are absent from the Lord” (which the number forty, as has been already said, mystically figures), we have need to celebrate the memory of the Lord’s Body, which we do in the Church, till He come.

verses.book: Sermons on Selected Lessons of the New Testament.
verses.text: Forasmuch, then as our Lord descended to this life, and “the Word was made flesh, that He might be delivered for our sins, and rise again for our justification,” Matthew followed the number forty; so that the one generation which there exceeds that number, either does not hinder its predominance—just as those thirty years do not hinder the perfect number of four hundred—or that it even has this further meaning, that the Lord Himself, by the addition of whom the forty-one is made up, so descended to this life to bear our sins, as yet, by a peculiar and especial excellency, whereby He is in such sense man, as to be also God, to be found to be excepted from this life.

verses.book: Sermons on Selected Lessons of the New Testament.
verses.text: For of Him only is that said, which never has been or shall be able to be said of any holy man, however perfected in wisdom and righteousness, “The Word was made Flesh.”

verses.book: Sermons on Selected Lessons of the New Testament.
verses.text: But Luke, who ascends up through the generations from the baptism of the Lord, makes up the number seventy-seven, beginning to ascend from our Lord Jesus Christ Himself through Joseph, and coming through Adam up to God.

verses.book: Sermons on Selected Lessons of the New Testament.
verses.text: And that is, because by this number is signified the abolition of all sins, which takes place in Baptism.

verses.book: Sermons on Selected Lessons of the New Testament.
verses.text: Not that the Lord Himself had any thing to be forgiven Him in baptism, but that by His humility He set forth its usefulness to us.

verses.book: Sermons on Selected Lessons of the New Testament.
verses.text: And though that was only the baptism of John, yet there appeared in it to outward sense the Trinity of the Father, the Son, and the Holy Ghost; and hereby was consecrated the Baptism of Christ Himself, whereby Christians were to be baptized.

verses.book: Sermons on Selected Lessons of the New Testament.
verses.text: The Father in the voice which came from heaven, the Son in the person of the Mediator Himself, the Holy Ghost in the dove.

verses.book: Sermons on Selected Lessons of the New Testament.
verses.text: Now, why the number seventy-seven should contain all sins which are remitted in Baptism, there occurs this probable reason, for that the number ten implies the perfection of all righteousness, and blessedness, when the creature denoted by seven cleaves to the Trinity of the Creator; whence also the Decalogue of the Law was consecrated in ten precepts.

verses.book: Sermons on Selected Lessons of the New Testament.
verses.text: Now the “transgression” of the number ten is signified by the number eleven; and sin is known to be transgression, when a man, in seeking something “more,” exceeds the rule of justice.

verses.book: Sermons on Selected Lessons of the New Testament.
verses.text: And hence the Apostle calls avarice “the root of all evils.”

verses.book: Sermons on Selected Lessons of the New Testament.
verses.text: And to the soul which goes a-whoring from God, it is said, in the Person of the same Lord, “Thou wast in hope, if thou didst depart from Me, that thou wouldest have something more.”

verses.book: Sermons on Selected Lessons of the New Testament.
verses.text: Because the sinner then has in his transgression, that is, in his sin, regard to himself alone—in that he wishes to gratify himself by some private good of his own (whence they are blamed “who seek their own, not the things which are Jesus Christ’s;” and charity is commended, “which seeketh not her own”); therefore, this number eleven, by which transgression is signified, is multiplied, not ten times, but seven, and so makes up seventy-seven.

verses.book: Sermons on Selected Lessons of the New Testament.
verses.text: For transgression looks not to the Trinity of the Creator, but to the creature, that is, to the man himself, which creature the number seven denotes.

verses.book: Sermons on Selected Lessons of the New Testament.
verses.text: Three, because of the soul, in which there is a kind of image of the Trinity of the Creator (for it is in the soul that man has been made after the image of God); and four, because of the body.

verses.book: Sermons on Selected Lessons of the New Testament.
verses.text: For the four elements of which the body is made up are known by all.

verses.book: Sermons on Selected Lessons of the New Testament.
verses.text: And if any one know them not, he may easily remember, that this body of the world, in which our bodies move along, has, so to say, four principal parts, which even Holy Scripture is constantly making mention of, East, and West, and North, and South.

verses.book: Sermons on Selected Lessons of the New Testament.
verses.text: And forasmuch as sins are committed either by the mind, as in the will only, or by the works of the body also, and so visibly; therefore the Prophet Amos continually introduces God as threatening, and saying, “For three and four iniquities I will not turn away,” that is,” I will not dissemble My wrath.”

verses.book: Sermons on Selected Lessons of the New Testament.
verses.text: Three, because of the nature of the soul; four, because of that of the body; of which two, man consists.

verses.book: Sermons on Selected Lessons of the New Testament.
verses.text: So, then, seven times eleven, that is, as has been explained, the transgression of righteousness, which has regard only to the sinner himself, make up the number seventy-seven, in which it is signified, that all sins which are remitted in Baptism are contained.

verses.book: Sermons on Selected Lessons of the New Testament.
verses.text: And hence it is that Luke ascends up through seventy-seven generations unto God, as showing that man is reconciled unto God by the abolition of all sin.

verses.book: Sermons on Selected Lessons of the New Testament.
verses.text: Hence the Lord Himself saith to Peter, who asked Him how oft he ought to forgive a brother, “I say not unto thee seven times, but until seventy times and seven.”

verses.book: Sermons on Selected Lessons of the New Testament.
verses.text: Now, whatever else can be drawn out of these recesses and treasures of God’s mysteries by those who are more diligent and more worthy than I, receive.

verses.book: Sermons on Selected Lessons of the New Testament.
verses.text: Yet have I spoken according to my poor ability, as the Lord hath aided and given me power, and as I best could, considering also the little time I had.

verses.book: Sermons on Selected Lessons of the New Testament.
verses.text: If any one of you be capable of anything further, let him knock at Him from whom I too receive what I am able to receive and speak.

verses.book: Sermons on Selected Lessons of the New Testament.
verses.text: But, above all things, remember this; not to be disturbed by the Scriptures, which you do not yet understand, nor be puffed up by what you do understand; but what you do not understand, with submission wait for, and what you do understand, hold fast with charity.
